_protected_html:
@@verbatim:0:5afa7f@@


The Principal Argument

In this text the notation @@math:12:3fc915@@ is used to designate an arbitrary argument of @@math:13:3de842@@ which means that @@math:14:449b75@@ is a set rather than a number. In particular, the relation @@math:9:aafc52@@ is not an equation, but expresses equality of two sets.

As a consequence, two non-zero complex numbers @@math:15:d0616b@@ and $r_2 (\cos \varphi_2 + i \sin\varphi_2)$ are equal if and only if

@@math:10:716ea1@@
where @@math:16:8e925e@@

In order to make the argument of @@math:17:7cc8c4@@ a well-defined number, it is sometimes restricted to the interval @@math:18:34fbdc@@ This special choice is called the principal value or the main branch of the argument and is written as @@math:19:30b6ec@@

Note that there is no general convention about the definition of the principal value, sometimes its values are supposed to be in the interval @@math:20:107323@@ This ambiguity is a perpetual source of misunderstandings and errors.

The principal value @@math:21:a1749e@@ of a complex number @@math:22:6e287d@@ is normally given by @@math:11:f908a7@@ where @@math:23:15bb18@@ is the slope, and @@math:24:cef949@@ converts slope to angle. But this is correct only when $x \neq 0,$ so the quotient is defined and the angle lies between @@math:25:c8d991@@ and @@math:26:4f6088@@ We need to extend this definition to cases where @@math:27:665109@@ is not positive, considering the principal value of the argument separately on the four quadrants.

The function @@math:28:aa0526@@ @@math:29:5c0fc2@@ is defined as follows:

@@math:5:3a62c6@@
Thus, if @@math:30:fc8172@@ with @@math:31:d7127a@@ and @@math:32:6d1ad9@@ then
@@math:6:8495c4@@

We can visualize the multiple-valued nature of @@math:33:30f923@@ by using Riemann surfaces. The following interactive shows some of the infinite values of @@math:34:a7cb45@@ Each branch is identified with a different color.

Arguments of products and quotients

If @@math:35:d167b3@@ and @@math:36:f826c6@@ then @@math:7:4337d1@@ implies @@math:8:0784a6@@ We can easily prove (\ref{arg-product}) by letting @@math:37:d6551a@@ and @@math:38:cd5131@@ denote any values of @@math:39:828f0b@@ and @@math:40:bcdb0e@@ respectively. Then expression (\ref{product}) tells us that @@math:41:559f2b@@ is a value of @@math:42:7c0ad3@@ If the values of @@math:43:738dd7@@ and @@math:44:e5e36c@@ are specified, those values correspond to particular choices of @@math:45:41ce2d@@ and @@math:46:fdfd3a@@ in the expressions @@math:47:4438dd@@ and @@math:48:56484e@@ Now, since \[ \left(\theta_1 + \theta_2\right)+ 2n\pi = \theta_1 + 2k\pi + \left[\theta_2 + 2(n-k)\pi\right], \] equation (\ref{arg-product}) is satisfied when we choose the value @@math:49:abfe4f@@ The verification when values of @@math:50:7f4e9e@@ and @@math:51:56f397@@ are specified follows by symmetry.

Statement (\ref{arg-product}) is sometimes valid when @@math:52:552d8f@@ is replaced everywhere by @@math:53:0a4f48@@ However, as the following example illustrates, that is not always the case.

Example: Consider @@math:54:0ca50d@@ and @@math:55:512b1e@@ Then @@math:56:7612ae@@ but @@math:57:b5fa1c@@ However, if we take the values of @@math:58:0b6ab3@@ and @@math:59:70a8e7@@ just used and select the value @@math:60:0d6d19@@ of @@math:61:443e0c@@ we find that equation (\ref{arg-product}) is satisfied.
